title: LFU Cache
description: Design LFU Cache using hashmap + DLL + heap

examples.input:
cache = LFUCache(2)
put(1, 10)
put(2, 20)
get(1)
put(3, 30)
get(2)
get(3)
examples.explanation:
1. Initialize cache capacity=2, entries=[]
2. put(1,10): entries=[(1,10,1,t1)], size=1
3. put(2,20): entries=[(1,10,1,t1), (2,20,1,t2)], size=2
4. get(1): scan finds (1,10,1,t1), increment freq to 2, return 10, entries=[(1,10,2,t1), (2,20,1,t2)]
5. put(3,30): cache full, scan for min freq: (2,20,1,t2) has freq=1 (lowest), evict it, add (3,30,1,t3), entries=[(1,10,2,t1), (3,30,1,t3)]
6. get(2): scan finds no key=2, return -1
7. get(3): scan finds (3,30,1,t3), increment freq to 2, return 30, entries=[(1,10,2,t1), (3,30,2,t3)]

examples.input:
cache = LFUCache(3)
put(1, 100)
put(2, 200)
get(1)
get(1)
put(3, 300)
put(4, 400)
get(2)

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n) per operation
approaches.summary: Use an array to store cache entries and scan it on each get/put to find the least frequently used item by tracking frequencies. This is straightforward but inefficient for large caches.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) for get and put operations

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force LFU Cache Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete LFU cache implementation using array scanning to find least frequently used items
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach implements an LFU cache by maintaining an array of cache entries, each containing key, value, and frequency count. On get operations, we scan the array to find the matching key, increment its frequency, and return the value. On put operations, if the cache is full, we scan the entire array to find the item with the minimum frequency (and optionally the oldest among ties), evict it, then add the new entry. The intuition is straightforward: we maintain all data in a simple linear structure and perform linear scans for all operations. This works because we're trading time efficiency for implementation simplicity - we can always find what we need by examining every element. The algorithm: 1) Initialize cache with capacity and empty array. 2) For get(key): scan array for matching key; if found, increment frequency, return value; else return -1. 3) For put(key, value): if key exists, update value and increment frequency; else if cache full, scan to find entry with minimum frequency (break ties by insertion order), remove it, then add new entry with frequency 1. Time: O(n) for both get and put due to linear scans. Space: O(n) for storing n entries. This feeds into understanding why more efficient approaches are needed - linear scans become prohibitively expensive for large caches. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle frequency ties properly, not updating frequency on cache hits, incorrect capacity management. Real pattern: use list of tuples (key, value, freq, timestamp).

examples.input:
cache = LFUCache(2)
put(1, 10)
put(2, 20)
get(1)
put(3, 30)
get(2)
get(3)
examples.trace:
1. Initialize cache capacity=2, entries=[]
2. put(1,10): entries=[(1,10,1,t1)], size=1
3. put(2,20): entries=[(1,10,1,t1), (2,20,1,t2)], size=2
4. get(1): scan finds (1,10,1,t1), increment freq to 2, return 10, entries=[(1,10,2,t1), (2,20,1,t2)]
5. put(3,30): cache full, scan for min freq: (2,20,1,t2) has freq=1 (lowest), evict it, add (3,30,1,t3), entries=[(1,10,2,t1), (3,30,1,t3)]
6. get(2): scan finds no key=2, return -1
7. get(3): scan finds (3,30,1,t3), increment freq to 2, return 30, entries=[(1,10,2,t1), (3,30,2,t3)]

examples.input:
cache = LFUCache(3)
put(1, 100)
put(2, 200)
get(1)
get(1)
put(3, 300)
put(4, 400)
get(2)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding and Evicting Least Frequently Used Item
ladders.desc: Scan array to identify item with minimum frequency for eviction when cache is full
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force LFU cache's eviction policy. When the cache reaches capacity and a new item needs to be inserted, we must identify which existing item to remove. The LFU policy dictates we remove the least frequently used item. The intuition: by scanning all entries and tracking the minimum frequency encountered, we can guarantee finding the item(s) with the lowest usage count. Since multiple items may share the same minimum frequency, we need tie-breaking - typically by removing the oldest among ties (LRU policy within same frequency). The algorithm: 1) Initialize min_freq = infinity, min_index = -1, oldest_time = infinity. 2) Iterate through all cache entries. 3) For each entry, compare its frequency with min_freq. If lower, update min_freq, min_index, and oldest_time to this entry's timestamp. 4) If frequency equals min_freq, compare timestamps - if this entry is older (smaller timestamp), update min_index and oldest_time. 5) After scanning, evict entry at min_index. Time: O(n) for scanning n entries. Space: O(1) for tracking variables. This feeds into the full put operation by providing the eviction logic. Common mistakes: not initializing min_freq properly, forgetting tie-breaking, incorrectly comparing timestamps. Real pattern: linear scan with tracking of best candidate.

examples.trace:
1. Initialize min_freq=∞, min_idx=-1, oldest=∞
2. Entry 0: (1,100,3,t1), freq=3 < ∞, update min_freq=3, min_idx=0, oldest=t1
3. Entry 1: (2,200,1,t2), freq=1 < 3, update min_freq=1, min_idx=1, oldest=t2
4. Entry 2: (3,300,2,t3), freq=2 > 1, skip
5. Entry 3: (4,400,1,t4), freq=1 == min_freq=1, compare timestamps: t4 > t2 (newer), keep current min_idx=1
6. Result: evict entry at index 1 (key=2)

1. Initialize min_freq=∞, min_idx=-1, oldest=∞
2. Entry 0: (5,50,2,t5), freq=2 < ∞, update min_freq=2, min_idx=0, oldest=t5
3. Entry 1: (6,60,2,t6), freq=2 == min_freq=2, compare timestamps: t6 > t5 (newer), keep min_idx=0
4. Entry 2: (7,70,2,t7), freq=2 == min_freq=2, compare timestamps: t7 > t5 (newer), keep min_idx=0
5. Result: evict entry at index 0 (key=5)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Linear Search with Frequency Tracking
ladders.desc: Scanning an array while maintaining running minimum frequency
ladders.explanation: This core logic demonstrates how to find an element with a specific property (minimum frequency) in an unsorted array using linear search. The intuition is simple but powerful: when data is unsorted and we need to find an extreme value (minimum or maximum), we must examine every element. By keeping a running 'best so far' value as we traverse, we can identify the target element in a single pass. For LFU cache, we need the minimum frequency, but with tie-breaking for timestamps. The algorithm: 1) Initialize best_freq to a very large number, best_index to -1, best_time to a very large number. 2) For each index i in array: get current entry's frequency f and timestamp t. 3) If f < best_freq: update best_freq=f, best_index=i, best_time=t. 4) Else if f == best_freq and t < best_time: update best_index=i, best_time=t. 5) After loop, best_index contains the target position. Time: O(n) for n elements. Space: O(1) for tracking variables. This feeds into the eviction sub-routine by providing the search mechanism. Common mistakes: using <= instead of < for timestamp comparison (which would select newer instead of older), not handling empty array case. Real pattern: single loop with conditional updates.

examples.trace:
1. Initialize best_freq=∞, best_idx=-1, best_time=∞
2. Index 0: freq=5 < ∞, update best_freq=5, best_idx=0, best_time=t1
3. Index 1: freq=3 < 5, update best_freq=3, best_idx=1, best_time=t2
4. Index 2: freq=3 == best_freq=3, compare t3 and t2: t3 > t2 (newer), keep current
5. Index 3: freq=7 > 3, skip
6. Result: index 1

1. Initialize best_freq=∞, best_idx=-1, best_time=∞
2. Index 0: freq=2 < ∞, update best_freq=2, best_idx=0, best_time=t10
3. Index 1: freq=2 == best_freq=2, compare t5 and t10: t5 < t10 (older), update best_idx=1, best_time=t5
4. Index 2: freq=4 > 2, skip
5. Result: index 1

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Conditional Logic
ladders.desc: Iterating through array elements and applying conditions to find specific elements
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches fundamental array traversal with conditional logic - a prerequisite for the linear search in LFU cache. The intuition: arrays store elements sequentially, and we can access each element by its index. When we need to find elements meeting certain criteria, we must examine each position and apply tests. For LFU, we need to test frequency values and timestamps. The algorithm: 1) Initialize tracking variables. 2) Use a for loop to iterate indices from 0 to n-1. 3) At each index, access the element and extract needed attributes. 4) Apply conditional checks (if statements) to compare with current best values. 5) Update tracking variables when conditions are met. 6) After loop, use the final tracked values. Time: O(n) for n elements. Space: O(1) for loop variable and trackers. This feeds into core logic by providing the iteration framework. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in loop bounds, not initializing trackers properly, confusing index with value. Real pattern: for i in range(len(array)): element = array[i]; if condition: update.

examples.trace:
1. Initialize found_idx = -1
2. i=0: value=1, 1>3? false
3. i=1: value=4, 4>3? true, set found_idx=1, break loop
4. Result: index 1

1. Initialize min_val=∞, min_idx=-1
2. i=0: val=5, 5<∞, update min_val=5, min_idx=0
3. i=1: val=2, 2<5, update min_val=2, min_idx=1
4. i=2: val=8, 8>2, skip
5. i=3: val=1, 1<2, update min_val=1, min_idx=3
6. i=4: val=3, 3>1, skip
7. Result: index 3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Modifying Array Elements
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of reading from and writing to array positions
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers the essential skills of working with arrays: accessing elements by index and modifying them. Arrays are contiguous blocks of memory where each element can be accessed in constant time using its index (position). The intuition: think of an array like a row of lockers numbered from 0 - you can go directly to locker #i without checking others. For reading: use array[index] to get the value. For writing: use array[index] = new_value to change it. In LFU cache, we need to read frequencies and timestamps, and update frequencies on cache hits. Key concepts: 1) Indexing starts at 0. 2) Must ensure index is within bounds (0 ≤ index < length). 3) Arrays have fixed size (in many languages) or dynamic size. Time: O(1) for access/modification. Space: O(n) for storing array. This feeds into array traversal by providing the element access mechanism. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing, accessing out-of-bounds indices, confusing index with value. Real pattern: val = arr[i]; arr[i] = new_val.

examples.input: Array = [10, 20, 30, 40]; Read index 2, then set index 1 to 25
examples.trace:
1. Initial array: [10, 20, 30, 40]
2. Read index 2: array[2] = 30
3. Set index 1 to 25: array[1] = 25
4. New array: [10, 25, 30, 40]

examples.input: Array = [5]; Read index 0, increment it by 3
examples.trace:
1. Initial array: [5]
2. Read index 0: value = 5
3. Modify: array[0] = 5 + 3 = 8
4. New array: [8]

testCases.input: array = [9]; read and write same index

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays as Sequential Storage
ladders.desc: Basic concept of arrays as ordered collections of elements accessible by position
ladders.explanation: This foundation introduces the array data structure - one of the simplest and most fundamental in computer science. An array is an ordered collection of elements where each element has a specific position called an index. Think of it like a train with numbered cars: car #0, car #1, etc., each holding some content. The key intuition: arrays provide direct access to any element if you know its position (index), but finding an element by value requires checking each position. Important characteristics: 1) Elements are stored contiguously in memory. 2) Indexing typically starts at 0. 3) Fixed size in many languages (must know capacity upfront). 4) All elements are usually the same type. Basic operations: create array, get element at index, set element at index, find length. In LFU cache, we use arrays to store cache entries in insertion order. Time: O(1) for access by index, O(n) for search by value. Space: O(n) to store n elements. This feeds into all higher levels by providing the fundamental storage structure. Common beginner mistakes: confusing index with value, thinking arrays start at 1, not understanding fixed size limitations. Real pattern: arr = []; or arr = [1,2,3]; x = arr[0]; arr[0] = 5.

examples.input: Create array with elements 10, 20, 30. What's at position 1?
examples.trace:
1. Create array: elements 10, 20, 30 in positions 0, 1, 2
2. Position 0: 10
3. Position 1: 20
4. Position 2: 30
5. Answer: position 1 contains 20

examples.input: Array [5, 8, 3]. Change middle element to 7. What's the new array?
examples.trace:
1. Initial array: positions 0=5, 1=8, 2=3
2. Middle element is position 1 (between 0 and 2)
3. Change position 1 from 8 to 7
4. New array: position 0=5, position 1=7, position 2=3
5. Result: [5, 7, 3]
